macro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Case of Rape and the Question of Rov (Majority) in Matters of Yichus

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: A raped child and Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri's position on majority status
The Gemara's initial question: How does Rov relate to the Rabban Gamliel / Rabbi Yehoshua debate?
The Gemara's answer: Rav Yehuda in the name of Rav—the case of the caravans of Tzipori

macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Kavuah (Fixed) and Parush (Separated): Why a Double Rov is Required

micro_segments.title: Explanation of why the caravans matter: The principle that the caravan is always parush
The risk of gezeirah (decree): Without the caravan, we might allow a case of kavuah
The principle of kol d'parish me-rubah parish versus kol d'kavuah k'mechtza al mechtza
The meat analogy: Found meat is parush; meat in your home is kveim
Application to the rape case: The distinction between separated men and fixed locations

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Kol D'Kavuah K'Mechtza Al Mechtza: Definition, Sources, and Scope

micro_segments.title: The classic case: Nine kosher stores and one non-kosher store; does the principle require a double rov?
Response to the question: The meat case as paradigm of a single rov, not double rov
The stringency of yichus (genealogy): Extra requirements beyond normal halakha
Nine dead rodents and one frog: The principle applies even when nine are problematic (kavuah l'kula)
The rule of reshus ha-yachid (private domain) versus reshus ha-rabbim (public domain) for doubts of tumah
Biblical source for kol d'kavuah: The case of random shooting into a group with one non-Jew

macro_segments.title: Reconciling the Mishnah with the Principle: Horat Sha'ah versus Double Rov

micro_segments.title: Two approaches to the Mishnah: Rav Chanan bar Rava says horat sha'ah (special circumstance)
Rabbi Yirmiya's challenge: If it's only a single rov, how can we rely on it for yichus?
The case of the foundling infant and the distinction between sustaining life (hachayas) versus yichus
The resolution: Either the Mishnah was a double rov case, or it was horat sha'ah—either way, don't draw the conclusion

macro_segments.title: The Foundling Infant: When Do We Assume Jewish Status and When Do We Maintain Doubt?

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Majority of non-Jews = non-Jew; majority of Jews = Jew; 50-50 = Jew
Rav's qualification: The 50-50 assumption is only for sustaining his life (hachayas), not for yichus
Shmuel's position: For pikuach nefesh (saving life), we do NOT go by rov—even a small doubt maintains Jewish status
The implication: Even if we've treated him as non-Jewish for years, we remember doubt for Shabbat violation
Rav Papa's examples: When majority is non-Jewish, we can feed him treifa; when majority is Jewish, we return lost objects
The case of ox goring: Different payments depending on Jewish or non-Jewish status; 50-50 requires proof

macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Next Mishna: The Widow or Divorced Woman Claiming to Have Been a Virgin at Marriage

micro_segments.title: The new question: First marriage versus second marriage, not first-night virginity
The signs of a first marriage: Hinuma (wedding carriage), uncovered head, flowing hair
Rabbi Yochanan ben Broka's view: Even distribution of parched grain is proof of a first wedding
Rabbi Yehoshua's concession: The principle of peh she'asar peh she'hitir (the mouth that forbids can permit)
